1: Psalm 101 is David's royal ethical manifesto — a king's vow about how he will govern his household and court. חֶסֶד-וּמִשְׁפָּט אָשִׁירָה לְךָ יְהוָה אֲזַמֵּרָה (ḥesed-ûmishpāṭ ʾāshîrāh lĕkā yĕhwāh ʾăzammêrāh, "I will sing of steadfast love and justice; to you, LORD, I will make music!") — the paired terms that will structure David's governance: ḥesed (steadfast love/loyalty — the covenantal commitment that goes beyond mere legal obligation) and mishpāṭ (justice — the right judgment that orders community life). These are Amos 5:24 ("let justice roll down like waters") and Hosea 12:7 ("maintain love and justice") in royal self-commitment. The lĕkā YHWH (to you, LORD) indicates that the king's ethical manifesto is addressed to God as its audience and source.
2: אַשְׂכִּילָה בְּדֶרֶךְ תָּמִים מָתַי תָּבוֹא אֵלָי (ʾaśkîlāh bĕderek tāmîm mātay tābôʾ ʾêlay, "I will conduct myself with wisdom and integrity. O when will you come to me?") — ʾaśkîlāh (hiphil: to act wisely, to have understanding — from śākal: the wisdom of successful moral living). Derek tāmîm: the blameless/whole way (the integrated path of those with no divided loyalties — Psalms 15:2; Psalms 18:23; Psalms 26:1). The sudden interruption mātay tābôʾ ʾêlay (O when will you come to me?) — the psalm's hidden lament: all the ethical resolve of vv1-8 is possible only when God is present. Without the divine coming, the manifesto is empty aspiration. אֶתְהַלֵּךְ בְּתָם-לְבָבִי בְּקֶרֶב בֵּיתִי (ʾethallêk bĕtom-lĕbābî bĕqereb bêtî, "I will walk with a blameless heart within my own house!") — integrity begins at home, not in public performance.
3: לֹא-אָשִׁית לְנֶגֶד עֵינַי דְּבַר-בְּלִיַּעַל (lōʾ-ʾāshît lĕneged ʿênay dĕbar-bĕliyyaʿal, "I will not set anything vile before my eyes!") — bĕliyyaʿal: worthlessness/wickedness (from bĕlî: without, and possibly yaʿal: profit — "that which is without profit"; or a compound meaning "without going up/ascending" — the depth of worthlessness). The visual gate of the heart: the king who controls what he looks at controls the direction of his desires. עֲשֵׂה-סֵטִים שָׂנֵאתִי לֹא יִדְבַּק-בִּי (ʿăśêh-sêṭîm śānêʾtî lōʾ yidbaq-bî, "I despise the conduct of those who fall away; it will not cling to me!") — sêṭîm: turnings-aside, deviations (from the straight way — the word for apostasy). Dābaq: to cling/stick fast (Ruth 1:14; Genesis 2:24: "a man shall cling to his wife").
4: לֵבָב עִקֵּשׁ יָסוּר מִמֶּנִּי רָע לֹא אֵדָע (lêbāb ʿiqqêsh yāsûr mimmenî rāʿ lōʾ ʾêdāʿ, "A crooked heart will depart from me; I will have nothing to do with evil!") — ʿiqqêsh: crooked, perverse (the opposite of yāshar/straight — Proverbs 2:15; Psalms 8:8; Deuteronomy 32:5: "a crooked and twisted generation"). Yāsûr mimmenî: will turn aside from me/will depart from me — the crooked heart will not find a home near the king. Rāʿ lōʾ ʾêdāʿ: evil I will not know (experiential knowledge — I will have no experience of, no acquaintance with). The progressive exclusion: nothing vile before the eyes (v3), nothing crooked in the heart (v4).
5: מְלוֹשְׁנִי בַסֵּתֶר רֵעֵהוּ אֹתוֹ אַצְמִית (mĕlôshĕnî bassêter rêʿêhû ʾōtô ʾaṣmît, "Whoever slanders his neighbor in secret — him I will silence!") — lāshan (piel: to use the tongue against — to slander, defame); bassêter: in secret (the covert character-assassination that takes advantage of absence). ʾAṣmît: I will cut off, silence (from ṣāmat: to make silent, to destroy — a judicial action by the king). גְּבַהּ-עֵינַיִם וּרְחַב לֵבָב אֹתוֹ לֹא אוּכַל (gĕbah-ʿênayim ûrĕḥab lêbāb ʾōtô lōʾ ʾûkhal, "The proud-eyed and the arrogant-hearted — I will not tolerate!") — gĕbah-ʿênayim: high eyes (the look of arrogance — Proverbs 6:17: "haughty eyes" first on the list of things God hates); rĕḥab lêbāb: broad of heart (the inflated self-importance that needs no correction). The king's court will have no room for those who tear down others or build themselves up.
6: עֵינַי בְּנֶאֱמְנֵי-אֶרֶץ לָשֶׁבֶת עִמָּדִי הֹלֵךְ בְּדֶרֶךְ תָּמִים הוּא יְשָׁרְתֵנִי (ʿênay bĕneʾĕmnê-ʾāreṣ lāshabet ʿimmādî hōlêk bĕderek tāmîm hûʾ yĕshārĕtênî, "My eyes will be upon the faithful of the land, that they may live with me; whoever walks the blameless way — he will serve me!") — where v5 described exclusion (the slanderer and the arrogant removed), v6 describes positive selection. Neʾĕmnê-ʾāreṣ: the faithful/trustworthy of the land (from ʾāman: to be firm, reliable — the same root as ʾāmên). Hōlêk bĕderek tāmîm: the one who walks the blameless way (the same phrase as v2 — what David commits to for himself, he also requires for those who serve him). The king's advisors and servants must reflect his own ethical commitment.
7: לֹא-יֵשֵׁב בְּקֶרֶב בֵּיתִי עֹשֵׂה רְמִיָּה (lōʾ-yêshêb bĕqereb bêtî ʿōśêh rĕmiyyāh, "No one who practices deceit will live in my house!") — rĕmiyyāh: deceit, treachery (from rāmāh: to beguile — Jeremiah 48:10; Micah 6:12). The royal household as a zone of integrity: those who practice deceit are excluded from the covenant space of the king's house. דֹּבֵר שְׁקָרִים לֹא-יִכּוֹן לְנֶגֶד עֵינָי (dōbêr shĕqārîm lōʾ-yikkôn lĕneged ʿênāy, "No one who speaks lies will stand before my eyes!") — shĕqārîm: lies (the plural covering habitual falsehood). Lōʾ yikkôn: will not be established/will not stand firm (the same root as the "firm establishment" of Psalms 93:1; Psalms 96:10). Truth is the condition of standing before the king.
8: לַבְּקָרִים אַצְמִית כָּל-רִשְׁעֵי-אָרֶץ לְהַכְרִית מֵעִיר יְהוָה כָּל-פֹּעֲלֵי אָוֶן (labĕqārîm ʾaṣmît kol-rishĕʿê-ʾāreṣ lĕhakhrit mêʿîr yĕhwāh kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen, "Each morning I will silence all the wicked of the land, cutting off every evildoer from the city of the LORD!") — labĕqārîm: each morning (the king's judicial session was in the morning — 2 Samuel 15:2: Absalom sat at the gate in the morning; Jeremiah 21:12: "administer justice every morning"). ʾAṣmît: I will cut off/silence (the repeated verb from v5 — the slanders is cut off; the wicked of the land are cut off). Mêʿîr yĕhwāh: from the city of the LORD (Jerusalem as the place of the divine presence — Psalms 48:1-2; Psalms 87:3). The psalm ends where it began: the king governs his own heart (vv2-4) and his household (vv5-7) so that the entire city of God may be purged of those who corrupt it.
